description: Figure 4 presents paired mechanical property charts for SPCC sheet steel, showing (a) stress–strain curves measured with a strain gauge up to 1 % strain and (b) the corresponding variation in the elastic modulus (stress–strain gradient) versus strain. The five curves labeled A–E represent distinct processing histories identified in the text: as-received, 15 % tensile pre-strained, pre-strained followed by low-temperature heat treatment (373 K for 90 h), annealed, and as-received with heat treatment. In (a), the pre-strained specimens display pronounced nonlinearity and a progressively decreasing slope within the nominally elastic region, while the annealed specimen maintains the highest stress level and the most linear response. Panel (b) quantifies this degradation: the 15 % pre-strained condition shows a continuous drop in modulus from initial loading, whereas the pre-strained and heat-treated specimen exhibits partial recovery where the gradient initially decreases and then rises toward the annealed level. The annealed specimen retains a high, nearly constant modulus (approximately 224 GPa initially, 206 GPa overall) until yielding. These trends confirm that the apparent elastic modulus reduction arises from mobile dislocations, and that low-temperature heat treatment can restore modulus stability via Cottrell atmosphere reformation.
